subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The position of center of mass of three masses $2$ kg, $3$ kg and $15$ kg placed with respect to mid point ($p$) of normal bisector, as shown in the figure is _______.
question: The position of center of mass of three masses $2$ kg, $3$ kg and $15$ kg placed with respect to mid point ($p$) of normal bisector, as shown in the figure is _______.
solution:
Let the midpoint $p$ of the normal bisector be the origin $(0,0)$.

The triangle is isosceles with two sides of $10\text{ m}$ and an included angle of $120^\circ$. The altitude from the $15\text{ kg}$ mass bisects the angle into two $60^\circ$ angles.

The length of the altitude is $10 \cos(60^\circ) = 5\text{ m}$.

Since $p$ is the midpoint of this altitude, the $15\text{ kg}$ mass is at a distance of $2.5\text{ m}$ above $p$, and the base is $2.5\text{ m}$ below $p$.

The distance of the $2\text{ kg}$ and $3\text{ kg}$ masses from the altitude is $10 \sin(60^\circ) = 5\sqrt{3}\text{ m}$.

The coordinates of the masses with respect to $p$ are:
$m_1 = 15\text{ kg}$ at $(0, 2.5)$
$m_2 = 2\text{ kg}$ at $(-5\sqrt{3}, -2.5)$
$m_3 = 3\text{ kg}$ at $(5\sqrt{3}, -2.5)$

The x-coordinate of the center of mass is:
$x_{cm} = \dfrac{m_1 x_1 + m_2 x_2 + m_3 x_3}{m_1 + m_2 + m_3} = \dfrac{15(0) + 2(-5\sqrt{3}) + 3(5\sqrt{3})}{15 + 2 + 3} = \dfrac{5\sqrt{3}}{20} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}$

The y-coordinate of the center of mass is:
$y_{cm} = \dfrac{m_1 y_1 + m_2 y_2 + m_3 y_3}{m_1 + m_2 + m_3} = \dfrac{15(2.5) + 2(-2.5) + 3(-2.5)}{20} = \dfrac{37.5 - 12.5}{20} = \dfrac{25}{20} = 1.25$

The position of the center of mass is $\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}, 1.25\right)$.

Answer: $\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}, 1.25\right)$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ falls freely from a height of $100 \mathrm{~m}$, on a platform of mass $3 \mathrm{~kg}$ which is mounted on a spring having spring constant $\mathrm{k}=1.25 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. The bodysticks to the platform and the spring's maximum compression is found to be $x$. Given that $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2},$ the value of $\mathrm{x}$ will be close to :
question: A body of mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ falls freely from a height of $100 \mathrm{~m}$, on a platform of mass $3 \mathrm{~kg}$ which is mounted on a spring having spring constant $\mathrm{k}=1.25 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. The bodysticks to the platform and the spring's maximum compression is found to be $x$. Given that $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2},$ the value of $\mathrm{x}$ will be close to :
solution: Velocity of $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ block just before it collides with $3 \mathrm{~kg}$ block $=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gh}}=\sqrt{2000} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Using principle of conservation of linear momentum just before and just after collision, we get $1 \times \sqrt{2000}=4 \mathrm{v} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{\sqrt{2000}}{4} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Initial compression of spring $1.25 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{x}_{0}=30 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}_{0} \approx 0$ using work energy theorem, $\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{g}}+\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{sp}}=\Delta \mathrm{KE}$ $\Rightarrow 40 \times x+\frac{1}{2} \times 1.25 \times 10^{6}\left(0^{2}-x^{2}\right)$ $=0-\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times v^{2}$ solving $x \approx 4 \mathrm{~cm}$

q: A body $A$ of mass $M$ while falling vertically downwards under gravity breaks into two parts; a body B of mass $1 / 3 \mathrm{M}$ and a body $\mathrm{C}$ of mass $2 / 3 \mathrm{M}$. The centre of mass of bodies $\mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ taken together shifts compared to that of body $\mathrm{A}$ towards
question: A body $A$ of mass $M$ while falling vertically downwards under gravity breaks into two parts; a body B of mass $1 / 3 \mathrm{M}$ and a body $\mathrm{C}$ of mass $2 / 3 \mathrm{M}$. The centre of mass of bodies $\mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ taken together shifts compared to that of body $\mathrm{A}$ towards
solution:
No horizontal external force is acting
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{cm}}=0 \\
& \text { since } \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{cm}}=0 \\
& \therefore \Delta \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{cm}}=0
\end{aligned}
$$

q: A body having specific charge 8 μC g-1 is resting on a frictionless plane at a distance 10 cm from the wall (as shown in the figure). It starts moving towards the wall when a uniform electric field of 100 V m-1 is applied horizontally towards the wall. If the collision of the body with the wall is perfectly elastic, then the time period of the motion will be ____s.
question: A body having specific charge 8 μC g-1 is resting on a frictionless plane at a distance 10 cm from the wall (as shown in the figure). It starts moving towards the wall when a uniform electric field of 100 V m-1 is applied horizontally towards the wall. If the collision of the body with the wall is perfectly elastic, then the time period of the motion will be ____s.
solution:
F=ma
qE=ma
a=qEm
Now d=12at2
t=2 da
t=2 dqEm

t=2×0.18×10-610-3×100=12
∴ Time period =2t=1 s
Answer =1.00

question: Three particles of masses 50 g, 100 g and 150 g are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side 1 m (as shown in the figure). The x, y coordinates of the centre of mass will be:

Coordinates of center of mass
xcm,ycm=50×0+150×12+100×150+150+100 m, 50×0+150×32+100×050+150+100 m

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Two bodies are having kinetic energies in the ratio 16:9. If they have same linear momentum, the ratio of their masses respectively is:
question: Two bodies are having kinetic energies in the ratio 16:9. If they have same linear momentum, the ratio of their masses respectively is:
solution:
The kinetic energy and the momentum of a moving object are related by

K=p22m   ...1

Hence, the ratio of the kinetic energies of two objects having masses m and m' can be calculated as follows:

KK'=p22mp22m'=m'm   ...2

Substitute the ratio of the kinetic energies into equation (2) and simplify to obtain the ratio of their masses.

⇒169=m'm⇒m:m'=9:16

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
What percentage of kinetic energy of a moving particle is transferred to a stationary particle when it strikes the stationary particle of 5 times its mass?
(Assume the collision to be head-on elastic collision)
question:
What percentage of kinetic energy of a moving particle is transferred to a stationary particle when it strikes the stationary particle of 5 times its mass?
(Assume the collision to be head-on elastic collision)
solution:
After collision,

Applying momentum conservation,

mu=mv1+5mv2  ⇒u=v1+5v2   ...1 

Coefficient of restitution, e=1=v2-v1u

∴ u=v2-v1   ...2

Adding  2u=6v2  ⇒v2=u3 and  u=u3-v1

∴ v1=u3-u=-2u3

Percentage change in kinetic energy =12mv12-12mu212mu2×100

=v12-u2u2×100=4u29-u2u2×100  =-59×100  =55.6%

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Four particles A, B, C  and D with masses mA=m,mB=2m,mC=3m  and mD=4m are at the corners of a square. They have accelerations of equal magnitude with directions as shown. The acceleration of the centre of mass of the particles is:
question: Four particles A, B, C  and D with masses mA=m,mB=2m,mC=3m  and mD=4m are at the corners of a square. They have accelerations of equal magnitude with directions as shown. The acceleration of the centre of mass of the particles is:
solution:
a→A=-ai^
a→B=aj^
a→C=ai^
a→D=-aj^
a→cm=maa→a+mba→b+mca→c+mda→dma+mb+mc+md
a→cm=-mai^+2maj^+3mai^-4maj^10m

=2mai^-2maj^10,=a5i^-a5j^=a5i^-j^

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The figure shows the position - time $(x-t)$ graph of one-dimensional motion of a body of mass $0.4 \mathrm{~kg}$. The magnitude of each impulse is
question: The figure shows the position - time $(x-t)$ graph of one-dimensional motion of a body of mass $0.4 \mathrm{~kg}$. The magnitude of each impulse is
solution:
From the graph, it is a straight line so, uniform motion. Because of impulse direction of velocity changes as can be seen from the slope of the graph.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Initial velocity }=\frac{2}{2}=1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \text { Final velocity }=-\frac{2}{2}=-1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& \qquad \bar{P}_{\mathrm{i}}=0.4 \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{s} \\
& \qquad \overline{\mathrm{P}}_{\mathrm{i}}=-0.4 \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{s} \\
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{J}}=\overline{\mathrm{P}}_{\mathrm{i}}-\overline{\mathrm{P}}_{\mathrm{i}}=-0.4-0.4=-0.8 \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{s}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{J}}=\text { impulse }) \\
& |\overrightarrow{\mathrm{J}}|=0.8 \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving at speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> collides elastically with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> at rest. After the collision, the two masses move at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with respect to the initial direction of motion of the body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>.</mo></math>. The largest possible value of the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>M</mi><mi>m</mi></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> for which the angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be equal, is :
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving at speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> collides elastically with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> at rest. After the collision, the two masses move at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with respect to the initial direction of motion of the body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>.</mo></math>. The largest possible value of the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>M</mi><mi>m</mi></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> for which the angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be equal, is :
solution:
Linear momentum conservation
(i) y-direction
mv2sinθ=mv1sinθ
mv2=mv1           ...(1)
(ii) x-direction
mv0=mv2cosθ+mv1cosθ            ...(2)
from (1) and (2)
mv0=mv2cosθ+mv2cosθ
mv0=2mv2cosθ       ...(3)
(iii) e=1given
v2-v1cos2θv0cosθ=1
v2-v1cos2θ=v0cosθ       ...(4)
v2-m22Mcos2θ=v0cosθ
v21-mMcos2θ=v0cosθ
mv02mcosθ1-mMcos2θ=v0cosθ
M-mcos2θ=2mcos2θ
M=2mcos2θ+m2cos2θ-1
Mm=4cos2θ-1
Mmmax=4-1=3

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Two blocks of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> are placed on the same straight line with coordinates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moved on the same line through a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> towards the other block. The distance through which the block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> must be moved to keep the position of centre of mass of the system unchanged is
question: Two blocks of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> are placed on the same straight line with coordinates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>cm</mi></math> respectively. The block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is moved on the same line through a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> towards the other block. The distance through which the block of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> must be moved to keep the position of centre of mass of the system unchanged is
solution:
Initial position of centre of mass: xCM=100+30×x40=3x4

Final position of the centre of mass: x'CM=106+30×x-y40=6+3x-y4

As position of centre of mass remains same,

xCM=x'CM ⇒3x4=6+3x-y4⇒3x=6+3x-3y  ⇒y=2 cm

Alternate method, ∆xCOM=m1∆x1+m2∆x2m1+m2⇒0=10×6+30y⇒y=-2 cm

q: Two particles of equal mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> have respective initial velocities <math><mi>u</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math><mi>u</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> . They collide completely inelastically. The energy lost in the process is:
question: Two particles of equal mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> have respective initial velocities <math><mi>u</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math><mi>u</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> . They collide completely inelastically. The energy lost in the process is:
solution:
Conserving momentum
mui^+mu2i^+u2j^=2mu1i^+u2j^
On solving
u1=3u4 and u2=u4
Change in K.E.
12mu2+12mu222-122m9u216+u216
=3mu24-5mu28=mu28

q: Two bodies of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> have position vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> respectively. The magnitude of position vector of centre of mass of this system will be similar to the magnitude of vector :
question: Two bodies of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> have position vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> respectively. The magnitude of position vector of centre of mass of this system will be similar to the magnitude of vector :
solution:
As we know, centre of mass is given by, r→com=∑mir→i∑mi

r→com=mı^+2ȷ^+k^+3m-3ı^-2ȷ^+k^m+3m

r→com=-8mı^-4mȷ^+4mk^4m=-2ı^-ȷ^+k^

The magnitude of the position vector of the centre of mass is 22+11+12=6.

It is similar to the magnitude of the option A.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Distance of the centre of mass of a solid uniform cone from its vertex is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. If the radius of its base is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and its height is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is equal to:
question: Distance of the centre of mass of a solid uniform cone from its vertex is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. If the radius of its base is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and its height is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is equal to:
solution:
Let the mass of solid cylinder is M and angle with vertical is θ. Now, we take a small element of thickness dy at a distance y from the vertex. The radius of small element is r, as shown in the figure below.

Then, tanθ=Rh=ry which will give, r=ytanθ. The density of the cone is given by,

ρ=M13πR2h=3MπR2h.

The mass of the small element is given by,

dM=ρdV

dM=3MπR2h×πr2dy=3My2tan2θR2hdy

The center of mass of the cone is given by,

z0=1M∫0hydM

z0=1M∫0hy·3My2tan2θR2hdy

z0=3R2hRh2y440h

z0=3h4.

q: A nucleus disintegrates into two nuclear parts, in such a way that ratio of their nuclear sizes is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Their respective speed have a ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is_________
question: A nucleus disintegrates into two nuclear parts, in such a way that ratio of their nuclear sizes is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><msup><mn>2</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Their respective speed have a ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is_________
solution:
Using the law of Momentum conservation

m1v1+m2(-v2)=0
⇒m1v1=m2v2
⇒m1m2=v2v1⇒4πρr3134πρr323=v2v1

It is given

 r1r2=1213
and r1r23=v2v1Therefore,12=v2v1
⇒v1v2=21

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: In a system two particles of masses $m_1=3 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=2 \mathrm{~kg}$ are placed at certain distance from each other. The particle of mass $m_1$ is moved towards the center of mass of the system through a distance $2 \mathrm{~cm}$. In order to keep the center of mass of the system at the original position, the particle of mass $m_2$ should move towards the center of mass by the distance _____ $\mathrm{cm}$.
question: In a system two particles of masses $m_1=3 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=2 \mathrm{~kg}$ are placed at certain distance from each other. The particle of mass $m_1$ is moved towards the center of mass of the system through a distance $2 \mathrm{~cm}$. In order to keep the center of mass of the system at the original position, the particle of mass $m_2$ should move towards the center of mass by the distance _____ $\mathrm{cm}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{X}_{\text {C.O.M. }}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_1 \Delta \mathrm{x}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \Delta \mathrm{x}_2}{\mathrm{~m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2} \\ & \Rightarrow 0=\frac{3 \times 2+2(-\mathrm{x})}{3+2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=3 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A piece of wood of mass <math><mn>0.03</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is dropped from the top of a <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> height building. At the same time, a bullet of mass <math><mn>0.02</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is fired vertically upward, with a velocity <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> from the ground. The bullet gets embedded in the wood. Then the maximum height to which the combined system reaches above the top of the building before falling below is: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A piece of wood of mass <math><mn>0.03</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is dropped from the top of a <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi></math> height building. At the same time, a bullet of mass <math><mn>0.02</mn><mi> </mi><mi>k</mi><mi>g</mi></math> is fired vertically upward, with a velocity <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> from the ground. The bullet gets embedded in the wood. Then the maximum height to which the combined system reaches above the top of the building before falling below is: <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><msup><mrow><mi>s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Using relative velocity



arel=0

vrel=100

Time t for collision,

100=vrel×t

t=100100=1 sec

vbullet=100-1×10=90m/s

vparticle=10×1=10m/s

S=100×1-12×10×1=95 m



Pi=Pf

⇒90×0.02-10×0.03=0.05V

V=30m/s⇒h=v22g=90020=45 m

So from top of building

45-5=40 m

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> at rest explodes into three pieces, in the ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Two smaller pieces fly off perpendicular to each other with velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The velocity of the third piece will be
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> at rest explodes into three pieces, in the ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. Two smaller pieces fly off perpendicular to each other with velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. The velocity of the third piece will be
solution:
Let the masses of the three parts be m', m' and 2m'.

Momentum of first part p1=m'×30=30m' and momentum of second part p2=m'×40=40m'.

Third part will fly in a direction opposite to the direction of resultant of p1 and p2, so that total momentum of the system after explosion remains zero.

Momentum of the third part is p3=p12+p22=30m'2+40m'2=50m'

Velocity of third part, v=50m'2m'=25 m s-1

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The centre of mass of a solid hemisphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the centre of the flat surface. Then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
question: The centre of mass of a solid hemisphere of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the centre of the flat surface. Then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
solution:
Centre of Mass of a solid hemisphere from centre of the  flat surface is given by,hcm=3R8

It is given that R= 8 cm

Therefore,

 x=hcm=3R8=3×88x=3 cm

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Consider two blocks A and B of masses $m_1=10 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=5 \mathrm{~kg}$ that are placed on a frictionless table. The block A moves with a constant speed $v=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ towards the block B kept at rest. A spring with spring constant $\mathrm{k}=3000 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is attached with the block B as shown in the figure. After the collision, suppose that the blocks A and B, along with the spring in constant compression state, move together, then the compression in the spring is, (Neglect the mass of the spring)
question: Consider two blocks A and B of masses $m_1=10 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_2=5 \mathrm{~kg}$ that are placed on a frictionless table. The block A moves with a constant speed $v=3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ towards the block B kept at rest. A spring with spring constant $\mathrm{k}=3000 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$ is attached with the block B as shown in the figure. After the collision, suppose that the blocks A and B, along with the spring in constant compression state, move together, then the compression in the spring is, (Neglect the mass of the spring)
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m}_1 \mathrm{v}_1+\mathrm{m}_2 \mathrm{v}_2=\left(\mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2\right) \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{cm}} \\ & \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{cm}} \Rightarrow \frac{10 \times 3}{10+5} \Rightarrow \frac{30}{15}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kx}^2=\frac{1}{2}(10)(3)^2-\left[\frac{1}{2}(15)(2)^2\right]$
$\Rightarrow 90-60=30=3000 x^2$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}^2 \Rightarrow \frac{30}{3000}=\frac{1}{100} \\ & \mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{10} \mathrm{~m}.\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A uniform rod of mass 250 g having length 100 cm is balanced on a sharp edge at 40 cm mark. A mass of 400 g is suspended at 10 cm mark. To maintain the balance of the rod, the mass to be suspended at 90 cm mark, is
question: A uniform rod of mass 250 g having length 100 cm is balanced on a sharp edge at 40 cm mark. A mass of 400 g is suspended at 10 cm mark. To maintain the balance of the rod, the mass to be suspended at 90 cm mark, is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \tau_{\text {Net }}=0 \Rightarrow(400 \mathrm{~g} \times 30)=(250 \mathrm{~g} \times 10)(\mathrm{mg} \times 50) \\ & \mathrm{m}=\frac{12000-2500}{50}=\frac{9500}{50} \\ & \mathrm{M}=190 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The distance of centre of mass from end <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> of a one dimensional rod <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> having mass density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>ρ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> (in meter) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>L</mi></mrow><mi>α</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is _____ (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is the distance form end <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>)
question: The distance of centre of mass from end <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> of a one dimensional rod <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> having mass density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>ρ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> (in meter) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>L</mi></mrow><mi>α</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is _____ (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is the distance form end <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>)
solution:
Given here:

Mass density, ρ=ρ01-x2L2 kg m-1

Let us suppose there is an element of length dx at a distance x from left must end.

Mass of this element is dm=ρdx=ρ01-x2L2

The centre of mass of this element is Xcm=∫xdm∫dm

=ρ0∫0lx-x2L2xdx∫0lρ01-x2L2dx

=L22-L44L2L-L33L2=L242L3=3L8

Hence, the value of α=8.

q: A nucleus disintegrates into two smaller parts, which have their velocities in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. The ratio of their nuclear sizes will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup></math>. The value of ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>’ is:
question: A nucleus disintegrates into two smaller parts, which have their velocities in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. The ratio of their nuclear sizes will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup></math>. The value of ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>’ is:
solution:
Given here, v1v2=32

Using conservation of momentum during disintegration, we have

m1v1=m2v2⇒m1 m2=23

Since, m=43πR3ρ 

We have m∝R3  ∵nuclear mass density, ρ=constant

m1m2=R1R23

⇒R1R2=2313

So, x=2.

q: A rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> has non-uniform linear mass density given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>l</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math><mi>a</mi></math> and <math><mi>b</mi></math> are constants and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>l</mi></math> The value of <math><mi>x</mi></math> for the centre of mass of the rod is at:
question: A rod of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> has non-uniform linear mass density given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>l</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math><mi>a</mi></math> and <math><mi>b</mi></math> are constants and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>≤</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mi>l</mi></math> The value of <math><mi>x</mi></math> for the centre of mass of the rod is at:
solution:
Xcm=1M∫0ιx.dM
dM=ρ.dx=a+bxı2.dx
xcm=∫xdM∫dm=∫xρdx∫ρdx=∫0ιxa+bx2ι2dx∫0ιa+bx2ι2dx
=ax220ι+bι2x440ιax0ι+bι2x330ι
=aι22+bι24aι+bι3=2a+b3a+bι4×3
=3ι42a+b3a+b

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Blocks of masses m, 2m, 4m and 8m are arranged in a line of a frictionless floor. Another block of mass m , moving with speed υ along the same line (see figure) collides with mass m in perfectly inelastic manner. All the subsequent collisions are also perfectly inelastic. By the time the last block of mass 8m starts moving the total energy loss is p% of the original energy. Value of ‘p ’ is close to:
question: Blocks of masses m, 2m, 4m and 8m are arranged in a line of a frictionless floor. Another block of mass m , moving with speed υ along the same line (see figure) collides with mass m in perfectly inelastic manner. All the subsequent collisions are also perfectly inelastic. By the time the last block of mass 8m starts moving the total energy loss is p% of the original energy. Value of ‘p ’ is close to:
solution:
Inelastic collision

mv=16 mv1

ΔK loss=12mv2−1216Mv162

=12mv2−12Mv216

=12mv21516

% ΔK loss=12mv2151612MV2×100=1516×100=93.75%

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A large block of wood of mass M=5.99 kg is hanging from two long massless cords. A bullet of mass m=10 g is fired into the block and gets embedded in it. The (block + bullet) then swing upwards, their center of mass rising a vertical distance h=9.8 cm before the (block + bullet) pendulum comes momentarily to rest at the end of its arc. The speed of the bullet just before the collision is: (Take g=9.8 m s-2)
question: A large block of wood of mass M=5.99 kg is hanging from two long massless cords. A bullet of mass m=10 g is fired into the block and gets embedded in it. The (block + bullet) then swing upwards, their center of mass rising a vertical distance h=9.8 cm before the (block + bullet) pendulum comes momentarily to rest at the end of its arc. The speed of the bullet just before the collision is: (Take g=9.8 m s-2)
solution:
From energy conservation,

[after bullet gets embedded till the system comes momentarily at rest]

M+mgh=12M+mv12
[v1 is velocity after collision] 

∴ v1=2σh

Applying momentum conservation, (just before and just after collision)

mv=M+mv1

v=M+mmv1=610×10-3×2×9.8×9.8×10-2

≈831.55 m s-1

q: A particle of mass m is moving along the x-axis with initial velocity ui^. It collides elastically with a particle of mass 10m at rest and then moves with half its initial kinetic energy (see figure). If  sin θ1=nsin θ2 then value of n is ________.
question: A particle of mass m is moving along the x-axis with initial velocity ui^. It collides elastically with a particle of mass 10m at rest and then moves with half its initial kinetic energy (see figure). If  sin θ1=nsin θ2 then value of n is ________.
solution:
From momentum conservation in perpendicular direction of initial motion.
mv1sinθ1=10mv2sinθ2
Given that 12mu212=12mv12  …….(2)
From equation (1) & (2)
sinθ1=10sinθ2
n=10
